report_metadata.cancer_type: External Auditory Canal Squamous Cell Carcinoma (EAC SCC)
report_metadata.stage: T3N0M0 (Modified Pittsburgh Stage III) — Middle Ear Involvement
molecular_profile: PD-L1 CPS status unknown — testing recommended

treatments.name: Lateral Temporal Bone Resection (LTBR) + Adjuvant IMRT (60-66 Gy)

evidence_level.rationale: No Phase 3 RCTs exist for this ultra-rare cancer (~1/million incidence). Evidence derives from multiple retrospective institutional series and systematic reviews/meta-analyses with consistent results across centers. The largest meta-analysis included 752 patients. Modified Pittsburgh staging validated across institutions.

survival_benefit.rationale: For T3N0M0 EAC SCC, LTBR + adjuvant RT yields 5-year OS of 50-67% and 5-year DSS of 87.5% in Stage III. Clear surgical margins are the strongest predictor of survival. Adjuvant RT improves outcomes significantly (p=0.02 for SCC patients). 5-year LRC of 66.2% with adjuvant RT.

accessibility.rationale: Available at major academic medical centers with skull base surgery programs. Requires specialized otologic/neurotologic surgical expertise. IMRT widely available. Prior mastoidectomy may complicate the surgical approach but does not preclude LTBR in experienced hands.

safety_profile.rationale: LTBR involves en bloc resection of the ear canal, tympanic membrane, malleus, and incus. Expected ipsilateral conductive hearing loss. Facial nerve preservation possible if not involved (T3 stage). Prior mastoidectomy alters anatomy and increases surgical complexity. RT side effects include fibrosis, osteoradionecrosis risk. IMRT reduces late toxicity vs 3D-CRT (8.7% vs 38.1% grade 2+ fibrosis).

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability — no biomarker requirements. Standard of care for all T3 EAC SCC regardless of molecular profile. Applicable to chronic otitis media-associated cases.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: En bloc surgical excision of the external auditory canal with surrounding temporal bone (lateral compartment), removing the bony and cartilaginous canal, tympanic membrane, malleus, and incus while preserving the stapes, facial nerve, and inner ear structures. Adjuvant intensity-modulated radiotherapy delivers 60-66 Gy to the tumor bed to eradicate microscopic residual disease and reduce local recurrence.

key_evidence.study_name: Temporal Bone Carcinoma – Treatment Patterns and Survival (Institutional series)

notable_side_effects: Facial nerve palsy risk (preservation attempted in T3)
Wound healing complications (especially with prior mastoidectomy)
treatments.availability: Available at academic medical centers with skull base surgery programs

treatments.name: Nivolumab Monotherapy (Recurrent/Platinum-Refractory Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: CheckMate-141 Phase 3 RCT demonstrated OS benefit in platinum-refractory R/M HNSCC. Additionally, a specific study examined nivolumab in 9 patients with platinum-refractory recurrent temporal bone SCC showing therapeutic effects similar to other HNSCC. FDA-approved indication.

survival_benefit.rationale: CheckMate-141: median OS 7.5 vs 5.1 months (HR 0.70, p=0.01). 24-month OS rate 16.9% vs 6.0%. Modest absolute benefit but nearly tripled long-term survival. Temporal bone SCC-specific data limited to 9 patients. Reserved for platinum-refractory recurrence.

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved for R/M HNSCC after platinum failure. Widely available. No requirement for EGFR or specific biomarker testing for access. Second-line standard of care in HNSCC.

safety_profile.rationale: Significantly fewer grade 3-4 AEs compared to standard chemotherapy (13.1% vs 35.1%). Maintains quality of life. Immune-related AEs manageable. No ototoxicity or nephrotoxicity — important for this patient with chronic otitis media.

biomarker_match.rationale: Approved regardless of PD-L1 status. Higher benefit in PD-L1 positive tumors. Temporal bone SCC-specific response data from limited case series. Universal SCC histology applicability.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Nivolumab is a fully human IgG4 anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the interaction between PD-1 on T cells and PD-L1/PD-L2 on tumor cells, thereby restoring anti-tumor T-cell function. In the second-line setting after platinum failure, it provides immune-mediated tumor control with durable responses in a subset of patients.
biomarker_requirements: None required (PD-L1 status informative but not mandatory)

treatments.name: Pembrolizumab + Cisplatin/5-FU (Recurrent/Metastatic Setting)

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-048 Phase 3 RCT is the gold-standard trial for R/M HNSCC first-line treatment. 5-year follow-up data published. FDA-approved combination. Extrapolation to EAC SCC recurrence is biologically rational given shared SCC histology.

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-048: Pembro+chemo median OS 13.0 months vs 10.7 months for cetuximab+chemo (HR 0.77). 5-year OS rate 16.0% vs 5.2%. Reserved for recurrent/metastatic setting — not first-line for T3N0M0 primary disease. Durable responses in responders (85% last >6 months).

accessibility.rationale: FDA-approved first-line for R/M HNSCC regardless of CPS. Widely available globally. Pembrolizumab and platinum/5-FU chemotherapy backbone is standard at all oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Combination carries cumulative toxicity from both immunotherapy and chemotherapy. Cisplatin ototoxicity critical concern with pre-existing chronic otitis media. Grade 3+ AEs in ~70%. Immune-related AEs plus chemotherapy myelosuppression. 5-FU mucositis and diarrhea.

biomarker_match.rationale: Approved for all CPS levels in combination with chemo. Greatest benefit in CPS >= 20 (HR 0.60). PD-L1 testing recommended but not required for combination regimen. Universal SCC applicability.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1) restores T-cell anti-tumor immunity while concurrent cisplatin induces DNA crosslinks and immunogenic cell death, and 5-fluorouracil inhibits thymidylate synthase. The combination leverages chemotherapy-induced tumor antigen release to enhance immune checkpoint blockade efficacy. Intended for recurrent or metastatic disease that is not amenable to curative surgery/RT.
biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 CPS testing recommended (benefit across all CPS levels with chemo combination)

treatments.name: Definitive Radiotherapy Alone (IMRT, 66-70 Gy)

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective series document outcomes of definitive RT for EAC SCC, typically reserved for surgically unfit or inoperable patients. Multi-institutional data available. Inferior to surgery+adjuvant RT in most analyses but provides locoregional control in select patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: Definitive RT inferior to surgery+adjuvant RT on multivariable analysis. 5-year OS approximately 40-50% for advanced-stage EAC SCC with definitive RT alone. 5-year LRC of 66.2%. May be appropriate if surgery contraindicated by prior mastoidectomy complications or patient preference.

accessibility.rationale: IMRT widely available at radiation oncology centers. No surgical expertise required. Outpatient treatment over 6-7 weeks. Avoids surgical morbidity. Available virtually everywhere.

safety_profile.rationale: Avoids surgical morbidity entirely. IMRT reduces late toxicity vs 3D-CRT (grade 2+ fibrosis 8.7% vs 38.1%). Risk of osteoradionecrosis of temporal bone. Radiation-induced hearing loss (ipsilateral). Skin reaction and fibrosis. No systemic toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability — no biomarker requirements. Applicable to all SCC histology. May be preferred option when chronic otitis media and prior mastoidectomy create hostile surgical field.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-dose intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) delivers 66-70 Gy in 33-35 fractions to the primary tumor volume, using inverse planning to generate highly conformal dose distributions that maximize tumor dose while sparing adjacent critical structures. DNA double-strand breaks in tumor cells lead to mitotic catastrophe and apoptosis. IMRT computer optimization allows complex dose painting around the temporal bone anatomy.

key_evidence.study_name: Definitive and adjuvant RT for EAC and temporal bone SCC: Long-term outcomes

treatments.availability: Widely available at radiation oncology centers

treatments.name: Concurrent Chemoradiotherapy with TPF (Docetaxel/Cisplatin/5-FU)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase I dose-finding trial specific to EAC SCC established recommended doses. Retrospective single-center study of 27 patients showed 3-year OS of 72.8%. No randomized comparison with surgery exists. TPF regimen extrapolated from established H&N SCC protocols (TAX323, TAX324).

survival_benefit.rationale: When combined with induction chemotherapy, 3-year OS reaches 90% in non-surgical patients (vs 0% without ICT, p=0.0075). For T3 disease specifically, 5-year OS of 100% reported with TPF-CRT in one Japanese series. Comparable to surgical outcomes in advanced disease.

accessibility.rationale: Cisplatin, docetaxel, and 5-FU are widely available chemotherapy agents. Concurrent CRT protocols available at most cancer centers. Does not require specialized skull base surgery expertise. May be preferred when prior mastoidectomy makes LTBR technically challenging.

safety_profile.rationale: TPF regimen carries significant toxicity: grade 3-4 neutropenia, mucositis, nephrotoxicity from cisplatin. Maximum tolerated dose of docetaxel in TPF-CRT for EAC is 40 mg/m2 (lower than standard H&N doses). Ototoxicity from cisplatin compounds existing hearing loss from chronic otitis media. Requires careful monitoring in 45yo patient.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirements. Universal applicability to all SCC histology. Particularly relevant for patients with chronic otitis media history where surgical anatomy is distorted.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Triple cytotoxic chemotherapy regimen: docetaxel (microtubule stabilizer), cisplatin (DNA crosslinker), and 5-fluorouracil (thymidylate synthase inhibitor) delivered concurrently with external beam radiotherapy (66-70 Gy). The chemotherapy radiosensitizes tumor cells while RT provides locoregional control. Induction TPF cycles precede concurrent CRT to reduce tumor volume.

key_evidence.study_name: Treatment outcomes of SCC of the EAC and potential benefit of induction chemotherapy followed by CRT

treatments.name: Cetuximab + Radiotherapy (Bioradiotherapy)

evidence_level.rationale: Bonner trial (Phase 3) demonstrated OS benefit for cetuximab+RT in locoregionally advanced HNSCC. Case reports document complete response in T4 EAC SCC treated with cetuximab+RT. NRG-HN004 Phase 2/3 evaluated cetuximab+RT vs durvalumab+RT in cisplatin-ineligible HNSCC patients.

survival_benefit.rationale: Bonner trial: median OS 49 months vs 29.3 months with RT alone. In EAC SCC case report, complete response maintained at 2 years with cetuximab+RT for T4 disease. Inferior to cisplatin-CRT in fit patients per RTOG 0522. Best reserved for cisplatin-ineligible patients or those refusing surgery.

accessibility.rationale: Cetuximab FDA-approved for H&N SCC with RT since 2006. Widely available. No renal toxicity concern (advantage over cisplatin in patient with potential hearing preservation goals). Weekly infusion protocol well-established.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than cisplatin-CRT. Main toxicity is acneiform skin rash (correlates with efficacy). No ototoxicity (important advantage for this patient with chronic otitis media and pre-existing hearing issues). No nephrotoxicity. Infusion reactions manageable with premedication. Radiation dermatitis may be enhanced.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR overexpressed in >90% of HNSCC. No companion diagnostic required for approved indication. EAC SCC shares EGFR expression patterns with mucosal HNSCC. KRAS mutations (which predict cetuximab resistance in CRC) are uncommon in HNSCC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Cetuximab is a chimeric IgG1 monoclonal antibody that binds the extracellular domain of EGFR with high affinity, blocking ligand binding and receptor activation. This inhibits tumor cell proliferation, survival, and angiogenesis. Combined with radiotherapy, cetuximab radiosensitizes EGFR-overexpressing tumor cells through inhibition of DNA damage repair pathways and cell cycle arrest.

key_evidence.study_name: Bonner Trial (Cetuximab+RT in locoregionally advanced HNSCC)

treatments.name: Perioperative Pembrolizumab (Neoadjuvant + Adjuvant) with Surgery/RT

evidence_level.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 Phase 3 RCT demonstrated EFS improvement in locally advanced HNSCC (HR 0.73, p=0.008). FDA-approved June 2025 for resectable LA-HNSCC with PD-L1 CPS>=1. No direct evidence in EAC SCC but strong biological rationale for extrapolation as SCC histology with shared molecular pathways.

survival_benefit.rationale: KEYNOTE-689: 36-month EFS 57.6% vs 46.4% (total population). Median EFS 51.8 vs 30.4 months. Major pathologic response in 9.4%. Higher R0 resection rates (89.1%). Extrapolation to EAC SCC uncertain but promising given shared SCC biology.

accessibility.rationale: Pembrolizumab FDA-approved for perioperative use in LA-HNSCC since June 2025. Off-label use for EAC SCC would require PD-L1 testing and multidisciplinary discussion. Insurance coverage may require prior authorization for off-label indication.

safety_profile.rationale: Grade 3+ AEs in 44.6% (comparable to control arm at 42.9%). Immune-related AEs include thyroiditis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, colitis. Does not add cisplatin-related ototoxicity. Treatment-related death 1.1%. Generally well-tolerated with manageable immune toxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires PD-L1 CPS >= 1 for approved indication. Benefit greatest in CPS >= 20 (HR 0.66). PD-L1 status unknown in this patient — testing recommended. Most HNSCC tumors express PD-L1, but EAC SCC expression data limited.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Pembrolizumab is an anti-PD-1 monoclonal antibody that blocks the PD-1/PD-L1 immune checkpoint pathway, restoring T-cell-mediated anti-tumor immunity. Administered neoadjuvantly (before surgery) to prime immune response and achieve pathologic downstaging, then adjuvantly (after surgery with or without RT) to eliminate micrometastatic disease and reduce recurrence. Perioperative approach leverages intact tumor antigen presentation before resection.
treatments.availability: FDA Approved for LA-HNSCC (June 2025); off-label for EAC SCC

treatments.name: Subtotal Temporal Bone Resection (STBR) + Adjuvant CRT

evidence_level.rationale: Retrospective series support STBR for T3 tumors with confirmed middle ear involvement. Multiple institutional series report outcomes. No randomized data comparing LTBR vs STBR for T3 disease, though LTBR associated with better survival in pooled analyses (p<0.01).

survival_benefit.rationale: STBR provides more extensive resection for middle ear involvement but carries inferior 5-year survival compared to LTBR in some series. Reserved for cases where LTBR margins would be inadequate. Adjuvant CRT improves locoregional control. 5-year OS approximately 40-50% for T3 with STBR.

accessibility.rationale: Requires highly specialized neurotologic surgical expertise available only at major skull base surgery centers. More complex operation than LTBR. Prior mastoidectomy significantly complicates the approach due to altered anatomy and potential adhesions.

safety_profile.rationale: More extensive surgery than LTBR with higher morbidity. Facial nerve sacrifice frequently required, necessitating nerve grafting and reanimation procedures. Complete ipsilateral hearing loss expected. Higher risk of CSF leak, meningitis, and wound complications. Prior mastoidectomy increases surgical risk further.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability for SCC with confirmed middle ear extension. No biomarker requirements. Decision based on imaging and intraoperative findings.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Extended surgical resection of the temporal bone including the entire external auditory canal, middle ear space, mastoid, and surrounding bone to achieve negative margins when tumor extends beyond the lateral temporal bone compartment. Followed by adjuvant chemoradiation to address microscopic disease. More aggressive than LTBR, sacrificing more temporal bone structures.

key_evidence.study_name: Surgical treatment for SCC of the temporal bone: predictors of survival

treatments.availability: Limited to major academic skull base surgery centers

treatments.name: Paclitaxel/Carboplatin/Cetuximab Induction + CRT (Modified TPF)

evidence_level.rationale: Introduced post-2021 at specialized centers for EAC SCC to reduce TPF toxicity. Used in the Frontiers in Oncology 2025 study as an alternative ICT regimen. Paclitaxel/carboplatin widely validated in HNSCC. Cetuximab addition supported by EXTREME trial data. Limited EAC-specific evidence.

survival_benefit.rationale: Part of the induction cohort achieving 3-year OS of 90% (combined with subsequent CRT) in EAC SCC non-surgical patients. Designed as less toxic alternative to TPF with similar efficacy. Carboplatin substitution avoids cisplatin-induced ototoxicity — critical for patient with chronic otitis media.

accessibility.rationale: All three agents commercially available and widely used. Carboplatin substitution well-accepted. Cetuximab FDA-approved for HNSCC. Standard chemotherapy infusion protocols. Available at most oncology centers.

safety_profile.rationale: Better tolerated than TPF: carboplatin less ototoxic and nephrotoxic than cisplatin (important for this patient). Paclitaxel neuropathy risk. Cetuximab rash. Reduced myelosuppression vs docetaxel. Still significant cumulative toxicity from triple-agent regimen.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR overexpression in >90% HNSCC provides rationale for cetuximab. No strict biomarker requirement. Particularly appropriate for patients needing to avoid cisplatin ototoxicity. Universal SCC applicability.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Induction regimen combining paclitaxel (microtubule stabilizer), carboplatin (DNA crosslinker with reduced ototoxicity vs cisplatin), and cetuximab (anti-EGFR monoclonal antibody). The triple combination provides cytotoxic tumor debulking while targeting EGFR-driven proliferation. Followed by concurrent chemoradiotherapy for definitive locoregional control. Designed to reduce treatment toxicity while maintaining efficacy of induction followed by CRT approach.

key_evidence.study_name: Treatment outcomes of SCC of the EAC (ICT cohort with modified regimen)

treatments.availability: All agents commercially available; off-label combination for EAC SCC

treatments.name: Proton Beam Therapy (Adjuvant or Definitive)

evidence_level.rationale: No prospective trials specific to EAC SCC with proton therapy. Evidence extrapolated from skull base tumor proton therapy data and HNSCC proton therapy studies. Dosimetric planning studies demonstrate superior dose conformality for temporal bone targets. Limited institutional case reports.

survival_benefit.rationale: Theoretical advantage from improved dose distribution — higher tumor dose with reduced dose to brainstem, contralateral cochlea, temporal lobe, and optic structures. May reduce late toxicity without compromising tumor control. No comparative survival data vs IMRT for EAC SCC specifically.

accessibility.rationale: Proton therapy centers limited globally (~40 in US). Higher cost than photon IMRT. Geographic constraints. Insurance coverage variable for this indication. May require travel to specialized center.

safety_profile.rationale: Bragg peak physics allows superior sparing of critical structures: brainstem, contralateral cochlea (hearing preservation), facial nerve, temporal lobe. Reduced late neurocognitive toxicity. Lower integral dose reduces second malignancy risk (relevant for 45yo). Pencil beam scanning reduces hearing loss risk.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability — no biomarker requirements. Applicable to all EAC SCC. Particularly relevant for young patients (45yo) where late toxicity reduction is clinically meaningful.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Proton beam therapy utilizes the Bragg peak physical property of proton particles, where radiation dose is deposited at a specific depth with rapid dose fall-off beyond the target volume. This allows highly conformal dose delivery to the temporal bone tumor bed while minimizing dose to adjacent critical structures (brainstem, cochlea, facial nerve, temporal lobe). Pencil beam scanning technology enables intensity-modulated proton therapy (IMPT) for complex skull base geometries.

key_evidence.study_name: Proton Therapy for Head and Neck SCC: Clinical Experience and Challenges

notable_side_effects: Acute skin reaction (similar to photon RT)
Reduced late fibrosis vs photon RT
Reduced ototoxicity compared to photon IMRT
treatments.availability: Available at proton therapy centers (~40 in US); limited global access

treatments.name: Cetuximab + Nivolumab (Combination Immunotherapy/Targeted)

evidence_level.rationale: Case report evidence of complete clinical remission in auricular SCC treated with cetuximab + nivolumab combination. No prospective trials for EAC SCC. Phase 2 data in HNSCC for EGFR inhibitor + checkpoint inhibitor combinations are emerging. Biologically rational combination.

survival_benefit.rationale: Case report showed complete remission in cutaneous SCC of the auricle. EGFR inhibition may enhance antigen presentation and T-cell infiltration, synergizing with anti-PD-1. Preclinical models support combination. Clinical benefit uncertain without prospective data.

accessibility.rationale: Both agents individually FDA-approved for HNSCC indications. Off-label combination use feasible with oncologist discretion. No specific regulatory approval for the combination in any cancer type. Insurance coverage uncertain.

safety_profile.rationale: Combined immune-related and EGFR-related toxicities. Skin rash may be enhanced. No ototoxicity or nephrotoxicity (advantage for this patient). Manageable safety profile based on limited case data. Risk of immune-related AEs comparable to nivolumab monotherapy.

biomarker_match.rationale: EGFR overexpression (>90% HNSCC) and PD-L1 expression both targetable. Dual-target approach may benefit broader patient population. No strict biomarker requirement for either agent in HNSCC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Dual-pathway blockade combining cetuximab (anti-EGFR, blocking tumor proliferation and enhancing antigen presentation/ADCC) with nivolumab (anti-PD-1, restoring T-cell anti-tumor immunity). Cetuximab-mediated ADCC may enhance tumor antigen release and cross-presentation, priming adaptive immune responses that are then amplified by PD-1 blockade. The combination targets both innate and adaptive anti-tumor immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Clinical Remission of Cutaneous SCC of the Auricle with Cetuximab and Nivolumab

treatments.name: High-Dose-Rate Intracavitary Brachytherapy (HDR-ICBT) Boost

evidence_level.rationale: Case report evidence of HDR intracavitary brachytherapy for EAC SCC. Established technique in other head and neck sites (nasopharynx, oral cavity). Technical feasibility demonstrated with custom applicators for ear canal geometry. Very limited published data.

survival_benefit.rationale: Brachytherapy boost can deliver high conformal dose to EAC tumor bed. May improve local control when combined with EBRT. Inverse square law dose fall-off spares deep structures. No comparative survival data vs EBRT alone for EAC SCC.

accessibility.rationale: Requires brachytherapy suite with HDR afterloader. Custom applicator fabrication needed for ear canal anatomy. Available at specialized radiation oncology centers. Technical expertise for temporal bone brachytherapy limited to few institutions.

safety_profile.rationale: Highly conformal dose delivery spares surrounding normal tissues. Acute mucosal reaction at treatment site. Risk of EAC stenosis. Procedure performed under anesthesia. Short treatment times minimize patient discomfort. Rapid dose fall-off protects facial nerve and cochlea.

biomarker_match.rationale: Universal applicability — no biomarker requirements. Radiation modality applicable to all SCC histology. Anatomy-dependent rather than biology-dependent technique.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: High-dose-rate intracavitary brachytherapy delivers focused radiation directly within the external auditory canal lumen using a custom-molded applicator. The Ir-192 or Co-60 source provides very high dose at the canal surface with rapid inverse-square-law dose fall-off, achieving high biologically effective dose to superficial tumor while sparing deeper structures (cochlea, facial nerve, brain). Used as a boost following external beam RT to intensify local treatment.

key_evidence.study_name: HDR intracavitary brachytherapy for SCC of EAC with EBRT for nodal coverage: case report

treatments.name: Volrustomig (Anti-PD-1/CTLA-4 Bispecific Antibody)

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 3 trial ongoing in locally advanced HNSCC (NCT06129864) as sequential therapy after definitive CRT. No data specific to EAC SCC. Bispecific antibody approach represents next-generation immunotherapy. Dual checkpoint blockade rationale supported by CTLA-4/PD-1 biology.

survival_benefit.rationale: No survival data yet — trial ongoing. Dual checkpoint blockade with ipilimumab+nivolumab has shown modest improvements in HNSCC. Bispecific format may improve therapeutic window. Benefit in ultra-rare EAC SCC entirely speculative.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through clinical trial (NCT06129864). Patient may qualify based on HNSCC diagnosis if trial accepts rare subsites. Limited trial sites. Investigational agent not commercially available.

safety_profile.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade historically associated with higher immune toxicity than single-agent anti-PD-1. Bispecific format may mitigate this vs combination of two separate antibodies. Grade 3+ immune AEs expected in 20-30%. No ototoxicity or nephrotoxicity.

biomarker_match.rationale: Targets both PD-1 and CTLA-4 pathways — broadly applicable to SCC. May benefit patients who would not respond to single-agent anti-PD-1. CTLA-4 blockade enhances T-cell priming in addition to PD-1-mediated T-cell reinvigoration.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Volrustomig is a novel PD-1/CTLA-4 bispecific antibody that simultaneously blocks two distinct immune checkpoint pathways. PD-1 blockade reinvigorates exhausted effector T cells in the tumor microenvironment, while CTLA-4 blockade enhances T-cell priming and depletes regulatory T cells. The bispecific format may achieve dual blockade with improved safety profile compared to combination of two separate antibodies.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase III trial of volrustomig in locally advanced HNSCC (NCT06129864)

notable_side_effects: Higher immune toxicity than single-agent PD-1 blockade

treatments.name: Zanzalintinib + Pembrolizumab (Multikinase Inhibitor + Anti-PD-1)

evidence_level.rationale: STELLAR-305 Phase 2/3 trial (NCT06082167) ongoing for first-line PD-L1-enriched R/M HNSCC. Zanzalintinib is a multikinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR, MET, and AXL. No EAC SCC-specific data. Mechanism biologically rational for vascular, invasive tumors.

survival_benefit.rationale: No survival data available — trial ongoing. Anti-angiogenic combinations with immunotherapy have shown promise in other solid tumors (kidney, liver). VEGFR/MET/AXL pathway relevant to SCC invasion and immune evasion. Benefit in EAC SCC speculative.

accessibility.rationale: Available only through STELLAR-305 clinical trial (NCT06082167). Limited enrollment sites. Zanzalintinib not commercially available. Patient would need R/M disease to qualify for current trial.

safety_profile.rationale: Multikinase inhibitors typically cause hypertension, hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, fatigue. Combined with pembrolizumab immune-related AEs. No specific ototoxicity expected. Safety profile from Phase 2/3 not yet fully characterized.

biomarker_match.rationale: Requires PD-L1 enriched tumor for trial eligibility. Targets VEGFR/MET/AXL pathways broadly expressed in SCC. Multi-target approach may be beneficial in heterogeneous tumors. AXL associated with treatment resistance in HNSCC.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Zanzalintinib is a novel multikinase inhibitor targeting VEGFR (anti-angiogenic), MET (anti-invasion/metastasis), and AXL (anti-immune evasion/resistance). Combined with pembrolizumab (anti-PD-1), the regimen aims to normalize tumor vasculature (improving immune cell infiltration), block tumor invasion pathways, and overcome adaptive resistance to checkpoint blockade. Multi-target approach addresses multiple hallmarks of cancer simultaneously.

treatments.name: Gedeptin Gene Therapy + Checkpoint Inhibitor

evidence_level.rationale: Phase 2 trial commencing 2025 for first-recurrence HNSCC. Novel gene therapy approach using adenoviral vector. No published efficacy data yet. Preclinical rationale for converting prodrug to cytotoxic agent selectively within tumors. No data in temporal bone or EAC SCC.

survival_benefit.rationale: No clinical survival data available. Mechanism of in-situ tumor destruction combined with immune activation theoretically promising. Phase 1 safety data suggests biological activity. Benefit in EAC SCC entirely speculative at this stage.

accessibility.rationale: Phase 2 trial only. Very limited enrollment. Novel gene therapy technology with specialized manufacturing and delivery requirements. Not expected to be commercially available for several years. Intratumoral injection required.

safety_profile.rationale: Intratumoral delivery limits systemic toxicity. Local injection site reactions expected. Combined with checkpoint inhibitor immune-related AEs. Gene therapy-specific risks (immunogenicity of viral vector). No ototoxicity or nephrotoxicity expected.

biomarker_match.rationale: No biomarker requirement for gene therapy component. Checkpoint inhibitor efficacy may correlate with PD-L1 status. Intratumoral approach bypasses need for systemic drug delivery to temporal bone. Applicable to accessible recurrent disease.

treatments.mechanism_of_action: Gedeptin utilizes an adenoviral vector to deliver the E. coli purine nucleoside phosphorylase (PNP) gene directly into tumor cells via intratumoral injection. The expressed enzyme converts the systemically administered prodrug fludarabine phosphate into a potent cytotoxic metabolite (2-fluoroadenine) selectively within the tumor. This in-situ tumor destruction releases tumor antigens, creating an immunogenic cell death effect that synergizes with concurrent checkpoint inhibitor therapy to generate systemic anti-tumor immunity.

key_evidence.study_name: Phase 2 trial of Gedeptin + ICI in first-recurrence HNSCC

clinical_trials.title: Phase III Trial of Volrustomig (Anti-PD-1/CTLA-4 Bispecific) as Sequential Therapy in Locally Advanced HNSCC After Definitive CRT
clinical_trials.early_results: No results yet — trial ongoing. Dual checkpoint blockade approach with novel bispecific format. May accept rare HNSCC subsites including EAC SCC.

clinical_trials.title: STELLAR-305: Zanzalintinib + Pembrolizumab vs Pembrolizumab + Placebo in First-Line PD-L1-Enriched R/M HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: No results yet — trial ongoing. Multikinase inhibitor (VEGFR/MET/AXL) + anti-PD-1 combination. Addresses immune evasion and angiogenesis simultaneously.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant and Adjuvant Pembrolizumab + SOC in Resectable Locally Advanced HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Positive: 36-month EFS 57.6% vs 46.4% (HR 0.73, p=0.008). FDA approved June 2025. Major pathologic response 9.4%. Higher R0 resection rates. Applicable to EAC SCC off-label.

clinical_trials.title: Phase 2 Trial of Gedeptin (Gene Therapy) + Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor in First-Recurrence HNSCC
biomarker_requirements: Accessible recurrent tumor for intratumoral injection
clinical_trials.early_results: Phase 1 established safety profile. Novel gene therapy mechanism converting prodrug to cytotoxin selectively in tumor. Immune-mediated abscopal effect proposed.

clinical_trials.title: Neoadjuvant Atezolizumab +/- Tiragolumab or Tocilizumab in Locally Advanced HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Multiple Phase 2 studies testing dual immune checkpoint blockade (PD-1 + LAG-3) and anti-PD-L1 + anti-TIGIT combinations in the neoadjuvant setting. May accept rare HNSCC subsites.

clinical_trials.title: Adjuvant Nivolumab After Surgery + RT in High-Risk Resected HNSCC
clinical_trials.early_results: Testing adjuvant checkpoint inhibitor therapy in resected HNSCC with high-risk features (positive margins, extranodal extension, perineural invasion). Relevant for EAC SCC with close/positive margins after LTBR.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Retrospective series (strongest available for EAC SCC)
combination_strategies.rationale: Surgery provides maximal locoregional control; adjuvant CRT addresses microscopic residual disease. LTBR + adjuvant RT demonstrated significant survival benefit (p=0.02) in SCC patients. Concurrent cisplatin radiosensitizes remaining tumor cells. Standard approach for T3N0M0 EAC SCC at most skull base centers. Prior mastoidectomy may complicate LTBR but does not preclude it.

Induction chemotherapy debulks tumor and treats micrometastatic disease before definitive CRT. In EAC SCC, ICT followed by CRT achieved 3-year OS of 90% vs 0% without ICT (p=0.0075). May serve as non-surgical alternative when prior mastoidectomy creates hostile surgical anatomy. TPF doses adjusted for EAC (docetaxel MTD 40 mg/m2).

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Phase 3 (KEYNOTE-689, extrapolated from HNSCC to EAC SCC)
combination_strategies.rationale: KEYNOTE-689 demonstrated EFS improvement with perioperative pembrolizumab in LA-HNSCC (HR 0.73). Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab may achieve pathologic downstaging before LTBR, potentially converting marginal cases to R0 resections. Adjuvant pembrolizumab prevents recurrence. FDA-approved for LA-HNSCC; off-label for EAC SCC but biologically rational given shared SCC histology.

Cetuximab-mediated ADCC releases tumor antigens, enhancing anti-PD-1 checkpoint blockade efficacy. Complete remission reported in auricular SCC. Cetuximab avoids cisplatin ototoxicity (critical for patient with chronic otitis media). Dual innate (ADCC) and adaptive (anti-PD-1) immune activation. No ototoxicity or nephrotoxicity from either agent.

combination_strategies.evidence_level: Dosimetric studies + clinical extrapolation from skull base tumors
combination_strategies.rationale: Proton therapy Bragg peak enables superior dose conformality around temporal bone surgical bed, sparing contralateral cochlea (hearing preservation), brainstem, and temporal lobe. Particularly advantageous in 45yo patient where late toxicity reduction maximizes quality-adjusted life years. Reduced integral dose lowers second malignancy risk over decades of survivorship.

supportive_care.evidence: Established clinical practice for post-temporal bone resection hearing rehabilitation. BAHA bypasses absent ear canal and ossicles to deliver sound via bone conduction to intact cochlea. Osseointegration achieved in 1-3 months post-implantation. Multiple device options available (percutaneous BAHA, transcutaneous OSIA, Bonebridge).
supportive_care.benefit: Restores functional hearing on the operated side after LTBR/STBR. Significant quality of life improvement. Enables binaural hearing when contralateral ear intact. May be implanted after radiation field has healed (typically 6-12 months post-RT). Critical for 45yo patient's functional and occupational rehabilitation.

supportive_care.approach: Facial Nerve Monitoring and Preservation Protocol
supportive_care.evidence: Intraoperative facial nerve monitoring is standard of care during LTBR and STBR. In T3 disease, facial nerve preservation is attempted when no clinical or radiographic evidence of nerve involvement. If sacrifice required, immediate cable nerve grafting (sural or great auricular nerve) or XII-VII jump graft provides best reanimation outcomes. Eye protection (gold weight, tarsorrhaphy) prevents corneal complications.
supportive_care.benefit: Preservation of facial function dramatically improves quality of life, psychological well-being, and social functioning. Immediate nerve grafting yields House-Brackmann grade III-IV recovery in most patients. Eye protection prevents exposure keratopathy. Critical concern for 45yo patient's long-term quality of life.

supportive_care.approach: Chronic Otitis Media Management and Infection Control
supportive_care.evidence: 30-year history of chronic otitis media is both a risk factor for EAC SCC (HR 10.39, p=0.003 for poor prognosis) and a perioperative concern. Pre-operative culture-directed antibiotic therapy to control active infection. Post-operative wound care critical with prior mastoidectomy cavity. Long-term surveillance for contralateral ear pathology.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced perioperative infection risk. Improved wound healing in previously operated temporal bone. Reduced risk of post-RT osteoradionecrosis in inflamed tissue. Optimized surgical field for LTBR. Culture-directed antibiotics more effective than empiric therapy in chronic suppurative otitis media.

supportive_care.approach: Ototoxicity Monitoring and Hearing Preservation Program
supportive_care.evidence: Cisplatin ototoxicity compounds pre-existing hearing loss from 30 years of chronic otitis media. Serial audiometry (baseline, mid-treatment, post-treatment, and every 3-6 months for 2 years) per ASHA guidelines. Carboplatin substitution reduces ototoxicity risk. Contralateral hearing preservation critical when ipsilateral hearing will be lost to surgery.
supportive_care.benefit: Early detection of ototoxicity allows dose modification or agent substitution. Preserves contralateral hearing for adequate long-term auditory function. Audiometric data guides hearing rehabilitation timing and device selection. 45yo patient has decades of hearing need ahead.

supportive_care.evidence: Temporal bone resection results in visible auricular and periauricular deformity. Loss of ear canal and potential pinna resection impacts body image and social functioning. Evidence-based cognitive behavioral therapy and peer support groups for head and neck cancer patients improve psychological adjustment. Prosthetic ear reconstruction or surgical auricular reconstruction options available.
supportive_care.benefit: Reduced depression and anxiety rates in H&N cancer patients receiving structured psychosocial support. Improved treatment adherence. Better quality of life scores. Auricular prosthesis or flap reconstruction addresses cosmetic concerns. Particularly important for younger (45yo) patient.

supportive_care.evidence: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy for temporal bone cancer may cause trismus, oropharyngeal mucositis, and dysphagia, though generally less severe than primary mucosal H&N sites. Preventive swallowing exercises (Therabite, tongue exercises) reduce long-term fibrosis. Dietitian consultation ensures adequate caloric intake during treatment.
supportive_care.benefit: Maintained nutritional status during CRT. Reduced treatment interruptions. Prevention of long-term trismus. Weight maintenance improves treatment tolerance and immune function. Particularly important if TPF induction chemotherapy causes nausea/anorexia.
